All we ask is that you pay the freight charges”</u_quote>. But that “gift” is a trap of credit, freight, and economic takeover. So capitalism is the mechanism, not the ultimate gift.

The ending reveals the real intended result. After Earth has bought out Zur’s businesses and governments, Broderick explains their goal is a society where Earthmen and Zurians become <u_quote>“equal partners”</u_quote>, and then states plainly: <u_quote>“That’s why we had to break down your caste system.”</u_quote>

This directly answers the question. The central transformation Earth brings is the destruction of Zur’s hereditary hierarchy, symbolized when Zotul realizes, <u_quote>“And that is why my brothers did not beat me when I failed!”</u_quote> The title points not to capitalism itself, but to the social upheaval Earth engineers: the end of the caste system.

results.argument:
The story’s final twist makes clear that the true “gift from Earth” is not capitalism as a system of trade, but Earth’s destruction of Zur’s rigid hereditary hierarchy. Early on, Zur is ruled by caste and family rank: Zotul is repeatedly beaten because he is the youngest brother and socially subordinate. At the end, Broderick explains Earth’s real mission after economically taking over the planet: <quote>“until the children of Earth and Zur are equal in knowledge and therefore equal partners. That's why we had to break down your caste system.”</quote>

That line directly answers the question. “Capitalism” is merely the mechanism Earth uses—corporations, credit, advertising, mortgages—to gain control. The result, and thus the “gift,” is social leveling. Zotul himself recognizes this when he notices a change in family behavior: <quote>“And that is why my brothers did not beat me when I failed!”</quote> Earth’s intervention dismantles the old order of inherited privilege and abuse. So the title points to answer A: the destruction of the caste system.

results.argument:
The title’s “gift from Earth” is best understood as the social transformation Earth ultimately brings to Zur: the end of its rigid hereditary caste order, not capitalism itself. In the story, capitalism is merely Earth’s tool—corporations, credit, freight charges, and mortgages are the means by which Earth gains control. But at the climax, Broderick explains the purpose behind that takeover. After buying out Zur’s businesses and governments, he tells Zotul that Earth will make the two peoples equal partners and states explicitly: <quote>“you'll be working for us ... until the children of Earth and Zur are equal in knowledge and therefore equal partners. That's why we had to break down your caste system.”</quote>

That line directly answers the question. Earlier, Earth’s stated mission is <quote>“to spread technology and prosperity throughout the Galaxy.”</quote> So capitalism is the delivery system for Earth’s expansion, but the “gift” to Zur—framed as progress—is the destruction of the old caste hierarchy. The ending makes answer A explicit; answer B confuses method with result.
